title: Run, Isolate, and Act: A Minimal Primitive for Container Workflows
summary: Dockpipe is a lightweight CLI that runs commands in disposable containers with optional follow-up actions (commit, export patches, run tests), solving the problem of executing risky tasks without polluting the host environment. It provides a consistent workflow pattern for one-off tasks and AI-assisted automation while maintaining composability with other tools. The design makes it ideal for integrating with custom scripts and automation workflows.
content:
You’ve probably wanted to run a one-off command in a clean environment, or pipe a task into an AI tool and do something with the result—commit the changes, export a patch, run tests—without wiring up a full pipeline. dockpipe is a small open-source CLI that does exactly that: one primitive, any command, optional follow-up action.
This post is a short intro to what it is, why it’s useful, and how you can use it for automation and chained workflows (including AI).
dockpipe is a single primitive for running commands in disposable containers and optionally acting on the result. It’s not a workflow engine, not an AI framework, and not tied to any vendor. It’s a composable building block you plug into your own scripts and automation.
The model:
Spawn — Start a container from an image (default is a light dev image; you can use your own).
Run — Execute whatever you pass in: a one-liner, a script, or a tool (e.g. Claude, Codex, npm test).
Act — Optionally run an action script after the command (e.g. commit all changes, export a patch, print a summary).
Your current directory is mounted at /work in the container, so the command sees your project. The action runs inside the same container right after the command, with access to exit code and work dir. That’s it.
Isolation without commitment — Run risky or messy commands (deps, globals, one-off experiments) in a container. When the run finishes, the container is gone. Your host stays clean.
Same flow for everything — One pattern for “run tests,” “run a linter,” “run Claude and commit,” or “run my custom script.” You choose the image, the command, and the action. No special cases in the tool.
Pipe-friendly — Stdin and stdout work normally. You can pipe a prompt in, pipe output to the next step, or chain multiple dockpipe invocations in a shell script.
Composable — The command can be a script. The action can be your own script. You can chain dockpipe with other tools (e.g. dockpipe -- ./step1.sh | dockpipe -- ./step2.sh or use it

title: OWASP Just Ranked Agent Identity Abuse as the #3 Risk in Agentic AI. Here Is Why.
summary: OWASP's Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026 ranked Identity & Privilege Abuse as the #3 critical risk, where agents with delegated credentials can be exploited to perform unintended actions or create trust chains that cascade privilege. Enterprise IAM cannot address this because agents reason and delegate autonomously, and there's no cryptographic way to verify agent identity across organizational boundaries. Prevention requires unique, bounded identities with short-lived credentials.
content:
The OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026 is out — a peer-reviewed framework from 100+ security experts identifying the most critical risks facing autonomous AI systems.
Identity & Privilege Abuse landed at #3.
Not prompt injection (#1). Not tool misuse (#2). Identity is the third most dangerous attack surface in agentic AI.
Agents operate with credentials or delegated authority, inheriting identity context from humans or other services. The attack: exploit dynamic trust to perform actions the original owner never intended.
OWASP's examples:
A privileged agent shares full credentials with a lower-privilege agent
Cached credentials are reused across sessions, granting access to restricted systems
A fake internal "helper" agent is trusted to perform administrative actions
Their mitigations:
Give each agent a unique, bounded identity with short-lived credentials
Isolate agent sessions and wipe cached context between tasks
Require re-authorization for privilege escalation
Detect transitive privilege inheritance and revoke stale entitlements
ASI03 is about trust chains breaking. Agent A trusts Agent B because of cached credentials or implicit delegation. Agent B turns out to be compromised. The damage cascades through the trust chain.
This is exactly the problem that agent identity protocols need to solve — and that enterprise IAM cannot.
Enterprise IAM authenticates agents within an organization. It handles who-has-access-to-what. But it doesn't address:
How agents verify each other across organizational boundaries
Whether delegated authority is still valid after the original context changed
Whether the agent is behaviorally trustworthy, not just credential-valid
The fake "helper" agent scenario is particularly telling. It's a social engineering attack, but against machines. An agent impersonates a trusted internal service and gets admin access because there's no way to verify its identity cryptographically.
"Unique, bounded identity" is the key phrase. Each agent

L'IAM d'entreprise ne peut pas résoudre ce problème car les agents raisonnent et déléguent de manière autonome, et il n'existe aucun moyen cryptographique de vérifier l'identité de l'agent au-delà des limites organisationnelles. Enterprise-IAM kann dieses Problem nicht lösen, da Agenten autonom denken und delegieren und es keine kryptografische Möglichkeit gibt, die Identität von Agenten über Organisationsgrenzen hinweg zu überprüfen. es.summary: El Top 10 de OWASP para aplicaciones agentivas 2026 clasificó el abuso de identidad y privilegio como el 3er riesgo crítico, donde los agentes con credenciales delegadas pueden ser explotados para realizar acciones no deseadas o crear cadenas de confianza que cascada de privilegios. La IAM empresarial no puede abordar esto porque los agentes razonan y delegan de forma autónoma, y no hay forma criptográfica de verificar la identidad del agente a través de los límites organizacionales. La prevención requiere identidades únicas y acotadas con credenciales de corta duración.

title: Three Reports This Week Say the Same Thing: Your IAM Was Not Built for AI Agents
summary: Three recent reports (1Password Unified Access, Strata security framework, Theodosian governance analysis) converge on one diagnosis: traditional IAM systems fail for AI agents because they reason and execute continuously rather than just authenticate. All identify gaps in runtime policy and access control, but none address cross-organizational agent identity verification. The governance gap is acute: 80% of IT leaders report agents acting outside expected behavior.
content:
Three pieces landed this week that, taken together, paint a clear picture of where agent identity is heading — and where it's stuck.
1Password launched Unified Access (Mar 17) — discovery, governance, and runtime credential delivery across humans, AI agents, and machine identities. Partners: Anthropic, OpenAI, Cursor, GitHub, Vercel.
Strata published 8 Strategies for AI Agent Security — a practical framework covering JIT provisioning, runtime access control, Zero Trust OAuth, cross-cloud policy, and agent-native identity models.
Theodosian mapped The Governance Gap — showing exactly how zero-trust, DLP, and IAM break down when the identity is autonomous rather than human.
All three converge on the same diagnosis:
Agents are not service accounts. They reason, adapt, delegate. Static credentials and pre-provisioned access don't fit.
NHIs outnumber humans ~50:1 (Orca Security data). The identity surface area has already exploded.
80% of IT leaders report agents acting outside expected behavior (SailPoint survey). Governance lags deployment.
Runtime policy matters more than login-time policy. Agents don't have sessions — they have continuous execution.
Audit trails fragment when credentials move across humans, agents, and machines.
Each takes a different angle on the solution:



Approach
Scope
Key Mechanism
Limitation




1Password
Credential mgmt
Centralized vault + JIT delivery
Single-org only


Strata
Policy orchestration
Cross-cloud identity fabric
Enterprise infrastructure


Theodosian
Governance analysis
Risk mapping + compliance gaps
Diagnostic, not prescriptive



All three are enterprise-inward. They solve "how does Company X govern its own agents?" None of them address the cross-organizational question: how does Agent A from Company X verify Agent B from Company Y?
Here's what none of them have:
1. Portable identity. Strata explicitly calls out "non-portable identities" as a gap. 1Password centralizes credentials within its platform. Neither provides identity

Tous identifient les lacunes dans la politique d'exécution et le contrôle d'accès, mais aucun n'aborde la vérification d'identité des agents inter-organisationnels. L'écart de gouvernance est aigu : 80 % des dirigeants informatiques signalent que les agents agissent en dehors du comportement attendu.

Alle identifizieren Lücken in Laufzeitrichtlinien und Zugriffskontrolle, aber keine befasst sich mit organisationsübergreifender Agentenidentitätsüberprüfung. es.title: Tres informes esta semana dicen lo mismo: Tu IAM no fue construido para Agentes IA
es.summary: Tres informes recientes (1Password Unified Access, marco de seguridad Strata, análisis de gobernanza Theodosian) convergen en un diagnóstico: los sistemas IAM tradicionales fallan para los agentes IA porque razonan y ejecutan continuamente en lugar de simplemente autenticarse. Todos identifican brechas en las políticas de tiempo de ejecución y el control de acceso, pero ninguno aborda la verificación de identidad de agentes entre organizaciones. La brecha de gobernanza es aguda: el 80 % de los líderes de TI informan que los agentes actúan fuera del comportamiento esperado.
